data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider DigitalOcean (DOCN) After A 188% One Year Rally?
data.description:

  • The stock last closed at US$90.01, with returns of 5.1% over 7 days, 62.5% over 30 days, 83.8% year to date, 188.2% over 1 year, 141.3% over 3 years and 123.6% over 5 years, which naturally raises questions about what is already reflected in the share price.
  • Recent headlines have focused on DigitalOcean's positioning in cloud infrastructure for smaller businesses and developers, along with ongoing interest in how its platform fits into broader software and hosting trends. This backdrop provides useful context when thinking about whether the current share price aligns with the underlying business.
  • DigitalOcean currently has a valuation score of 1 out of 6, so the sections that follow compare different valuation methods and, at the end, introduce a more complete way to think about what the stock could be worth.

DigitalOcean Holdings scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Approach 1: DigitalOcean Holdings Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects a company’s future cash flows and then discounts them back to today’s dollars to estimate what the business might be worth right now.

For DigitalOcean Holdings, the model uses last twelve months Free Cash Flow of about $136.7 million and applies a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. Analyst inputs and extrapolations feed into ten year projections, moving from $192.2 million of projected Free Cash Flow in 2026 to $521.7 million in 2035, all expressed in US$ and then discounted to today using Simply Wall St’s assumptions.

Combining these projected cash flows produces an estimated intrinsic value of about $55.00 per share using this DCF framework. Compared with the recent share price of $90.01, the implied DCF result suggests the stock is 63.7% overvalued on this model alone.

Result: OVERVALUED

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to relate what you are paying per share to the earnings that each share generates. It links the share price directly to current earnings, which is usually the core driver of long term returns for many investors.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how quickly earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings are. Higher expected growth and lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while lower growth or higher risk often correspond to a lower P/E.

DigitalOcean Holdings currently trades on a P/E of 36.09x. This is above the broader IT industry average P/E of 19.93x and below the peer group average of 57.17x. data.title: Partners Group Weighs Healthcare Expansion And Tech Deal To Reshape Growth
data.description:
• Partners Group Holding (SWX:PGHN) has appointed Pete Zippelius as Co-Head of its Private Equity Health & Life vertical, sharpening its focus on healthcare and life sciences.
  • The firm is also reported to be in early discussions to acquire a significant stake in Happiest Minds Technologies alongside other private equity investors.

Partners Group Holding, trading at around CHF866.0, has seen mixed share performance, with a 4.2% gain over the past week and 5.6% over the past month, set against a 15.9% decline year to date and a 22.7% decline over the past year. Over a 3-year horizon the stock shows a 14.9% return, while the 5-year return sits at a 20.6% decline, highlighting a bumpy journey for long-term holders.

For investors watching SWX:PGHN, the push into healthcare alongside potential exposure to a technology player such as Happiest Minds Technologies reflects a broader effort to refine where capital is deployed. How these moves relate to future positioning and resilience will depend on execution, the quality of underlying assets and how well these themes fit with Partners Group Holding's existing portfolio mix.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Partners Group Holding by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Partners Group Holding.

4 things going right for Partners Group Holding that this headline doesn't cover.

For Partners Group, the combination of a healthcare push and possible M&A activity in a listed software company fits its core role as a diversified private-markets investor. The reported interest in a controlling stake in Happiest Minds Technologies, which is focused on IT services and artificial intelligence projects, would tilt more capital toward digital and AI-related themes. At the same time, the appointment of Pete Zippelius as Co Head of Private Equity Health & Life points to deeper sector specialization in healthcare and life sciences. Together, these moves could broaden the firm’s deal pipeline, help support fee-earning assets across cycles, and potentially strengthen relationships with institutional clients that want exposure to both healthcare and technology. However, a large controlling stake in a listed company and a growing healthcare platform can increase operational complexity and execution risk, especially when commercial due diligence is still underway and any transaction outcome is uncertain. For you as a shareholder or potential investor, a key question is whether these types of deals improve the balance between growth opportunities and the risks already flagged around high debt levels and dividend coverage.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Partners Group Holding Narrative

  • The push into healthcare and a potential stake in an AI-focused software company lines up with the narrative of broader access to private markets and diversified asset exposure across sectors and regions.
  • Large, complex transactions and sector-specific platforms could add to the operational complexity that the narrative already highlights as a risk to margins if cost efficiencies do not materialise.
  • The potential role in a Southeast Asia logistics REIT tied to e-commerce infrastructure, together with healthcare and technology transactions, may not be fully captured in earlier commentary that focused more on private credit, infrastructure, and evergreen wealth products.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Partners Group Holding to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Any acquisition of a controlling stake in Happiest Minds Technologies would increase execution and integration risk at a time when analysts already flag operational complexity and high debt as concerns.
  • ⚠️ A larger footprint in healthcare and AI-focused technology could concentrate exposure in sectors that can be sensitive to regulation, competition from players such as KKR, Blackstone, or Carlyle, and changing client appetite.
  • 🎁 A healthcare build-out led by an experienced Co Head, combined with technology and logistics-related deals, could support diversified earnings streams and help underpin recurring fee income.
  • 🎁 These initiatives may support rewards already highlighted by analysts, including expectations for earnings growth and the view that the shares trade below some estimates of fair value.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, focus on whether the Happiest Minds Technologies discussions progress beyond early-stage due diligence, the structure and size of any eventual deal, and how Partners Group finances a potential acquisition. Watch how quickly the expanded Health & Life vertical starts to translate into new transactions and whether management provides clear targets around capital deployment, fee-earning assets, and costs. It is also worth tracking updates on the proposed Southeast Asia logistics REIT tied to JD.com, because this would give more colour on Partners Group’s approach to listed vehicles and e-commerce infrastructure exposure.
